question.text: Which of the following correctly describes an interim dividend?

answer.text: A dividend that is declared but never actually paid to shareholders
A dividend declared by the Board of Directors during the financial year before the annual accounts are finalized
A dividend paid only to preference shareholders during the year
A dividend declared at the Annual General Meeting after audited results

explanation.text: **The Logic:** An interim dividend is declared by the Board of Directors during the financial year, typically on a quarterly basis, before the annual accounts are finalized. Board approval alone is sufficient for interim dividends, unlike final dividends which require AGM approval. **The Trap:** Many candidates confuse interim and final dividends. Final dividend is declared at the AGM after year-end; interim dividend can be declared anytime during the year by the Board. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series V-A covers dividend distribution and its impact on mutual fund NAV (ex-dividend date adjustments). **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Track quarterly board meeting dates for companies in client portfolios -- interim dividends are typically announced along with quarterly results.
